Today started with such promise, the morning sun streaming through my window made me feel alive and grateful. I had a productive meeting at work where my ideas were well received, and my colleague even complimented my presentation skills. Later I went for a walk in the park and just felt at peace with the world.

I have been struggling with my mental health for months now. Every day feels like a battle just to get out of bed. My friends don't seem to understand what I'm going through, and I feel like I'm slowly fading away from everyone's life. The worst part is not knowing if this feeling will ever end.

I am absolutely livid about what happened at work today. My manager threw me under the bus in front of the entire team for a mistake that wasn't even mine. Then when I tried to explain, they cut me off. I work so hard for this company and this is how they repay my loyalty. This place is toxic.

The anxiety has been building for weeks and today it finally peaked. I have three major deadlines next week, a family event I'm supposed to organize, my car needs repairs I can't afford, and my landlord just raised the rent. I feel like I'm drowning in responsibilities and there's no way to keep my head above water.

This past month has been the most exhausting period of my life. Between working double shifts, taking online courses at night, caring for my elderly parents on weekends, and trying to maintain some semblance of a social life, I'm spread dangerously thin. My body aches, my mind is foggy, and I can't remember the last time I truly rested.

I've been doing a lot of thinking lately about where my life is heading. Three years ago I was certain about my career path, but now I'm questioning everything. Am I really passionate about engineering or was it just my parents' dream? What would I do if money wasn't a factor? These questions keep spinning in my mind without resolution.

Despite the rain and the traffic and the long queue at the coffee shop, today was unexpectedly wonderful. A stranger held the door for me, my old school friend called out of nowhere, and I found a beautiful handwritten note my wife left in my lunchbox. Sometimes the smallest gestures carry the most weight.

The grief comes in waves. Some days I think I'm doing better and then a song plays or I see someone who looks like her and the pain crashes back. Three months since she left and the apartment still smells like her perfume. I keep her coffee mug exactly where she left it because moving it feels like erasing her.

I sat through an entire dinner listening to my relatives criticize every choice I've ever made: my job, my apartment, my hobbies, even my friends. The passive-aggressive comments and backhanded compliments were relentless. By dessert I was clenching my fists under the table trying not to explode because I'm sick of being everyone's punching bag.

I stare at my phone waiting for the callback. They said they'd let me know by Friday. It's now Saturday evening. Every notification makes my heart skip a beat. I've been refreshing my email obsessively. This job means everything to me. If I don't get it, I don't know what my plan B is. The uncertainty is eating me alive.

Three consecutive night shifts this week and my body is officially giving up. My hands were shaking today during rounds. I accidentally called a patient by the wrong name because my brain just won't function anymore. I drove home with the windows down in the cold just to stay awake. This isn't sustainable.

Walking through the old neighborhood today stirred up a complicated mix of feelings. The playground where we used to play, the bakery that closed down, the tree we carved our initials into. Everything changes and nothing changes. I'm not sad exactly, just aware of time passing and wondering what it all adds up to in the end.

My daughter took her first steps today and in that two-second wobble across the living room, every sleepless night, every diaper change at 3am, every moment of doubt about being a good parent melted away. I picked her up and held her close, crying tears of pure unadulterated joy. This is what life is about.

I woke up at 3am in a cold sweat again. The nightmare about the accident keeps coming back. During the day I'm fine but at night my brain replays everything in vivid detail. The screech of tires, the impact, the silence after. My therapist says it's normal processing but it doesn't feel normal. I'm afraid to close my eyes.

Today I sat in the park for two hours just watching people. Couples walking hand in hand, kids playing on swings, elderly people feeding pigeons. Everyone seems to have a purpose, a connection. I'm just sitting here trying to figure out where I fit in this picture. Not particularly sad or happy. Just thinking.
